intro:
Jesus Christ is the eternal Son of God become man — the central figure of Scripture and the foundation of the Christian faith. "Jesus" (the Greek form of Joshua, YHWH saves) names his saving mission; "Christ" (Greek for the Hebrew Messiah, the Anointed One) names his office as the promised King, Priest, and Prophet. Historic Christianity confesses him as one person in two natures — truly God and truly man — not a demigod or merely an inspired teacher, but God the Son who took our humanity to redeem it.

Before his birth he eternally existed as the Word: "In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God… all things were made through him" (John 1:1–3); he is "the image of the invisible God" who holds all creation together (Colossians 1:15–17; Hebrews 1:3). In the incarnation "the Word became flesh" (John 1:14), born of the virgin Mary in Bethlehem as Immanuel, "God with us" (Matthew 1:18–23; Luke 2:11). He was tempted in every way yet without sin (Hebrews 4:15).

His ministry proclaimed the kingdom of God (Mark 1:15): he called the Twelve, taught with unique authority, worked miracles, welcomed sinners, and declared that all the Scriptures point to him (Luke 24:27; John 5:39). He came "not to be served but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many" (Mark 10:45). Crucified under Pontius Pilate as the atoning sacrifice for sin foretold by Isaiah (Isaiah 53:5; 1 Corinthians 15:3–8), he rose bodily on the third day, "declared to be the Son of God in power… by his resurrection from the dead" (Romans 1:4) — prompting Thomas’s confession, "My Lord and my God!" (John 20:28).

Risen, he ascended and was made "both Lord and Christ" (Acts 2:36), poured out the Holy Spirit, and now reigns until he returns to judge the living and the dead (Acts 1:11; Philippians 2:9–11). He is the one mediator between God and humanity (1 Timothy 2:5), and "there is salvation in no one else" (Acts 4:12); he himself is "the way, and the truth, and the life" (John 14:6). His full deity and full humanity in one person were confessed at the Council of Chalcedon (AD 451).

sections.title: The Person of Christ
sections.body:
The question of the person of Christ — who he is — is the central Christological question of the NT and the object of two millennia of doctrinal reflection. Jesus himself raised the question explicitly: 'Who do people say the Son of Man is?... But what about you? Who do you say I am?' (Matt. 16:13-15). The Gospels present Jesus making claims that presuppose divine authority: forgiving sins (Mark 2:5-7), accepting worship (Matt. 28:17; John 20:28), identifying himself with YHWH's 'I AM' (John 8:58; cf. Ex. 3:14), and claiming a unique mutual knowledge with the Father (Matt. 11:27; John 10:30). His use of titles — Son of Man (drawing on Dan. 7:13-14), Son of God, Lord (Kyrios) — each conveyed different dimensions of his identity.

The early church's confessional development articulated the implications of Jesus's resurrection: the Pauline christological hymns (Phil. 2:6-11; Col. 1:15-20) identify the pre-existent Son as the agent and heir of all creation who emptied himself in incarnation. John's Logos prologue (John 1:1-18) makes the identification of the incarnate Jesus with the pre-existent Word explicit. The ecumenical councils from Nicaea (325) to Chalcedon (451) refined the dogmatic formulations — the Son is of the same substance (homoousios) as the Father; two natures, one person; unconfused, unchanged, undivided, inseparable — in response to proposals that failed to honor either Christ's full deity or full humanity. The result is the classical Christology shared across Roman Catholic, Eastern Orthodox, and Reformation Christianity as the irreducible core of faith in Jesus.

sections.title: The Offices of Christ (Prophet, Priest, King)
sections.body:
The doctrine of Christ's three offices (munus triplex) organizes his saving work under the categories of prophet, priest, and king — the three roles in Israel for which individuals were anointed with oil. As prophet, Christ declared God's word with supreme authority: Moses had promised a prophet greater than himself (Deut. 18:15-18), and Jesus fulfilled this in his teaching ministry (Acts 3:22), performing signs authenticating his message, and being himself the divine Word incarnate. The prophetic office extended through the Spirit's ongoing guidance of the church and will be consummated in the final revelation (Rev. 1:1).

As priest, Christ mediated between God and humanity through his sacrifice and intercession. Where the Levitical priests offered animals repeatedly, Christ offered himself once and entered the heavenly sanctuary with his own blood, obtaining eternal redemption (Heb. 9:12, 24). His intercession continues perpetually: 'he always lives to intercede for them' (Heb. 7:25). As king, Christ was installed at the right hand of the Father at his ascension (Ps. 110:1; Acts 2:33-36), exercises dominion over all creation and the church (Eph. 1:20-22; Col. 2:15), and will return to consummate his kingdom over every hostile power (1 Cor. 15:24-28; Rev. 19:11-16). The three offices together provide the comprehensive framework within which Calvin (Institutes 2.15) and subsequent Reformed theology organized the whole of Christ's redemptive work as the fulfillment of Israel's three anointed roles.

sections.title: Kingly Office of Christ
sections.body:
The kingly office of Christ is one of the three munus (offices) of Christ—prophet, priest, and king—a threefold classification developed in Reformed theology to systematize the work of the Messiah. The anointing of prophets, priests, and kings in the Old Testament was understood as a typological prefiguring of the one Messiah (anointed one) who would fulfill all three roles. Christ's kingly office refers to his exercise of sovereign authority over the universe, his church, and his enemies.

Scripture describes Christ's kingship in several dimensions: the eternal, divine reign he shares with the Father; the mediatorial kingship given to the incarnate Son (Psalm 2; Matthew 28:18, all authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me); and the eschatological consummation when he delivers the kingdom to the Father after defeating all enemies including death (1 Corinthians 15:24–28). The present exercise of his reign takes place primarily through the gospel and the Holy Spirit, and the church is the outpost of his kingdom in the world. The confession Jesus is Lord (Romans 10:9; 1 Corinthians 12:3) is fundamentally a declaration of his kingly authority over the believer's life.

sections.title: Humiliation of Christ
sections.body:
The humiliation of Christ refers to the voluntary condescension of the eternal Son of God in the incarnation and throughout his earthly life and death. The classical theological category of humiliatio is grounded in Philippians 2:5–11, where Paul describes Christ as being in the form of God, yet not regarding equality with God as something to be grasped, but emptying himself (ekenōsen), taking the form of a servant, being made in the likeness of humans, and being obedient to death—even death on a cross. This self-emptying (kenosis) is the doctrinal basis for the theology of humiliation.

Reformed and Lutheran theologians systematized Christ's humiliation as encompassing: (1) his incarnation and birth in humble circumstances; (2) his subjection to the Law; (3) his sufferings; (4) his death; and (5) his burial and descent to the realm of the dead. Each aspect represented a further stage in the voluntary condescension of one who was by nature Lord of all. The humiliation is inseparable from and is the precondition for the exaltation: the one who descended is the one who ascended above all heavens (Ephesians 4:10), and the name above every name is given precisely to the crucified one (Philippians 2:9–11).

sections.title: Obedience of Christ
sections.body:
The obedience of Christ is a systematic theological category distinguishing his active obedience (lifelong positive fulfillment of the law's demands) from his passive obedience (his suffering of the law's penalty for sin). The distinction, developed especially in Reformed federal theology, draws on Romans 5:12-21, where Paul contrasts 'the one trespass' of Adam with 'the one act of righteousness' of Christ, and 'the disobedience of the one man' with 'the obedience of the one man' (5:18-19). Christ's obedience is the ground of justification: because he perfectly obeyed the law as the last Adam, his righteousness is reckoned to those united to him by faith.

The active obedience encompasses Jesus's lifelong submission to the law — circumcised on the eighth day, presented at the temple, observing feasts, 'fulfilling all righteousness' at his baptism (Matt. 3:15), resisting every temptation, keeping every precept. The passive obedience encompasses his submission to suffering and death as the penalty due to sinners, not to himself: he 'became obedient to death — even death on a cross' (Phil. 2:8). Hebrews 5:8-9 states the paradox: 'Although he was a son, he learned obedience from what he suffered and, once made perfect, he became the source of eternal salvation for all who obey him.' The obedience of Christ accomplishes what humans could not — a perfect active righteousness credited to those united to him by faith, and a perfect passive submission that bore their penalty — making it central to the doctrine of imputed righteousness in both Lutheran and Reformed soteriology.

sections.title: Intercession of Christ
sections.body:
The intercession of Christ is one dimension of his ongoing high priestly work: having completed his atoning sacrifice on earth, the ascended Christ lives to intercede for his people before the Father in heaven. Hebrews 7:25 states that "he is able to save to the uttermost those who draw near to God through him, since he always lives to make intercession for them." This perpetual intercession is grounded in his finished sacrifice (Hebrews 9:12, 24) — he entered the heavenly sanctuary "by his own blood" and appears in the presence of God on behalf of his people.

Romans 8:34 places Christ's intercession alongside his death, resurrection, and exaltation at God's right hand as grounds for the believer's assurance: "who is to condemn? Christ Jesus is the one who died — more than that, who was raised — who is at the right hand of God, who indeed is interceding for us." John 17 records the high priestly prayer in which Jesus intercedes for his disciples and for all who will believe through their word. The First Epistle of John identifies Christ as the believer's advocate (parakletos) with the Father (1 John 2:1), using legal language that overlaps with the intercessory role described in Hebrews.

sections.title: Baptism of Christ
sections.body:
The baptism of Jesus by John in the Jordan River marks the inauguration of Jesus's public ministry and is recorded in all four Gospels. Matthew 3:13–17 presents the fullest account, including Jesus's explanation to John — suffer it to be so now: for thus it becometh us to fulfil all righteousness — in response to John's hesitation to baptize the one greater than himself. At the moment of Jesus's baptism, the heavens opened, the Spirit descended as a dove, and a divine voice declared: This is my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased — the trinitarian disclosure that frames the entire subsequent ministry.

Jesus also uses the term baptism metaphorically in Luke 12:50 to describe his coming passion: I have a baptism to be baptized with; and how am I straitened till it be accomplished! This baptism of suffering — his death and burial — corresponds in Paul's theology to the believer's baptism into Christ's death and resurrection (Romans 6:3–4). The baptism of Christ thus functions both as a historical inaugural event and as a theological type of the death and new life that Christian baptism signifies.

sections.title: Nativity of Christ
sections.body: The nativity of Christ — the birth of Jesus of Nazareth — fulfilled multiple prophetic predictions: that the Messiah would be born of a virgin (Isa. 7:14; Matt. 1:22–23), that his birthplace would be Bethlehem of Judah (Mic. 5:2; Matt. 2:6), and that the flight to Egypt and the slaughter of Bethlehem's children would occur (Hos. 11:1; Jer. 31:15; Matt. 2:15–18). Luke records the birth in a manger because there was no room in the inn (Luke 2:7), the announcement to shepherds by angels, and the visit of the magi (recorded in Matthew 2). The date of the nativity is uncertain; early church fathers proposed dates in spring or autumn, and December 25 was established as the Western liturgical observance by the 4th century. The incarnation — God taking on human nature in Jesus — is theologically central to both soteriology (Christ as the second Adam) and Christology (the hypostatic union of divine and human natures).

sections.title: Temptation of Christ
sections.body:
The Temptation of Christ is narrated in Matthew 4:1-11 and Luke 4:1-13, with a brief reference in Mark 1:12-13. Immediately after his baptism and the divine declaration of sonship, Jesus was 'led by the Spirit into the wilderness to be tempted by the devil' (Matt. 4:1). The forty days echo Israel's forty years in the wilderness, and each of Satan's three temptations presupposes a distorted use of divine power, countered by Jesus with quotations from Deuteronomy. The sequence in Matthew: (1) turning stones to bread — provision apart from the Father's timing, countered with Deut. 8:3: 'man does not live on bread alone'; (2) casting himself from the pinnacle of the temple — presumptuous testing of divine protection, Satan citing Ps. 91:11-12, countered with Deut. 6:16: 'do not put the LORD your God to the test'; (3) worshipping Satan in exchange for the kingdoms of the world — idolatry for a shortcut to messianic rule, countered with Deut. 6:13: 'worship the LORD your God, and serve him only.'

The temptation narrative is theologically important in multiple respects. It demonstrates that Jesus was genuinely tempted as a human being — the author of Hebrews insists that he 'has been tempted in every way, just as we are — yet he did not sin' (Heb. 4:15; cf. 2:18), making his sympathetic high priesthood dependent on this genuine human experience of testing. The typological comparison with Israel is explicit: where Israel failed God's tests in the wilderness (grumbling for bread, testing God at Massah, worshipping the golden calf), Jesus as the obedient Israel succeeds — not by extraordinary divine power but by faithful use of Scripture and submission to the Father's will. Luke's note that the devil departed 'until an opportune time' (4:13) indicates that temptation continued throughout Jesus's ministry, reaching its climax in Gethsemane (Luke 22:28).

sections.title: Revelation of Christ
sections.body:
The Revelation of Christ (Greek apokalypsis Iesou Christou) refers in New Testament usage both to the initial disclosure of Christ in the incarnation and to his future visible appearing at his second coming. Paul uses the phrase for both: "the grace of God which was given you by Jesus Christ" (1 Corinthians 1:4–7) anticipates his readers waiting for "the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ" and his revelation. Most distinctively, 2 Thessalonians 1:7 promises that God will give rest to those who suffer "when the Lord Jesus shall be revealed from heaven with his mighty angels, in flaming fire taking vengeance on them that know not God."

This future appearing — the parousia and apokalypsis — is depicted as unmistakable and universal, contrasting sharply with the hiddenness of Christ's first coming. The entire orientation of the early church was shaped by expectation of this revelation: "looking for the blessed hope and the glorious appearing of the great God and our Saviour Jesus Christ" (Titus 2:13).

sections.title: Genealogy of Jesus Christ
sections.body: The New Testament preserves two genealogies of Jesus: Matthew 1:1–17 and Luke 3:23–38. Matthew opens his Gospel with a descending genealogy from Abraham through David and the royal line of Judah to Joseph, the legal father of Jesus, structured in three sets of fourteen generations as a mnemonic device highlighting the Davidic messianic credentials of Christ. Luke's genealogy ascends from Joseph back through David, Abraham, and ultimately to Adam and God, emphasizing the universality of Christ's significance for all humanity. The two genealogies diverge between David and Joseph, and various solutions have been proposed — the most common being that Matthew traces Joseph's legal lineage while Luke traces Mary's biological ancestry, or that one represents a levirate marriage succession. Both evangelists affirm that Jesus was born of a virgin and that Joseph was his legal but not biological father (Matt. 1:16; Luke 3:23), situating his lineage within the covenant promises made to Abraham and David while maintaining the uniqueness of his birth.

sections.title: Trial of Jesus
sections.body:
The trial of Jesus proceeded through multiple stages spanning Jewish religious and Roman civil jurisdictions over a single night and morning. After his arrest in Gethsemane, Jesus was taken first to Annas, the former high priest and father-in-law of the current high priest Caiaphas (John 18:12-14, 19-23). He was then brought to Caiaphas and the Sanhedrin, where testimony was sought to justify a capital charge (Matt. 26:57-68; Mark 14:53-65). False witnesses offered inconsistent testimony about destroying the temple. Finally the high priest asked directly: 'Are you the Messiah, the Son of the Blessed One?' Jesus's affirmative answer — with explicit reference to Daniel 7:13 — was declared blasphemy, and the Sanhedrin voted for death. A morning session may have represented a formal ratification of the night's verdict (Luke 22:66-71).

Since the Sanhedrin lacked authority under Roman occupation to execute (John 18:31), Jesus was brought to the Roman governor Pontius Pilate. Pilate found no basis for the charge (Luke 23:4), sent him to Herod Antipas (Luke 23:6-12), and on his return offered to release him under the Passover amnesty — but the crowd demanded Barabbas. Pilate's declaration 'I find no basis for a charge against this man' (John 18:38; 19:4, 6) and his ultimate capitulation to crowd pressure fearing accusation before Caesar (John 19:12-16) are consistent with Roman provincial judicial practice under political pressure. The charge affixed to the cross — 'King of the Jews' (Matt. 27:37) — was the Roman charge of sedition. Early Christian interpretation saw both Jewish and Roman complicity in the trial as fulfilling prophetic patterns while noting that the primary initiative lay with the Jerusalem religious leadership (Acts 2:23; 4:27-28).
